title: Linked Lists
description: Linked lists test pointer manipulation and in-place algorithms. FAANG interviewers love them because bugs are subtle. Master Floyd's cycle detection, reversal, and the slow/fast pointer technique.

sections.title: Implementation & Basic Operations
sections.description: A singly linked list node holds a value and a next pointer. No random access — traversal is O(N). Insertion/deletion at a known node is O(1). Always check for None before accessing .next.

sections.title: Reversal — In-Place & Recursive
sections.description: Reversing a linked list in-place uses three pointers: prev, curr, next. O(N) time, O(1) space. The recursive version is elegant but uses O(N) stack space — mention this tradeoff.
sections.codeBlock:
# Iterative — O(N) time, O(1) space
def reverse(head):
    prev, curr = None, head
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next   # save next
        curr.next = prev  # reverse link
        prev = curr       # advance prev
        curr = nxt        # advance curr
    return prev           # prev is new head

# Recursive — O(N) time, O(N) stack space
def reverse_recursive(head):
    if not head or not head.next:
        return head
    new_head = reverse_recursive(head.next)
    head.next.next = head   # node after us points back to us
    head.next = None        # we become the new tail
    return new_head

# Reverse sublist [left, right] (1-indexed) — LeetCode 92
def reverse_between(head, left, right):
    dummy = ListNode(0, head)
    pre = dummy
    for _ in range(left - 1): pre = pre.next  # go to node before left

    cur = pre.next
    for _ in range(right - left):
        nxt = cur.next
        cur.next = nxt.next
        nxt.next = pre.next
        pre.next = nxt
    return dummy.next

sections.title: Floyd's Cycle Detection
sections.description:
Floyd's 'tortoise and hare' algorithm detects cycles in O(N) time, O(1) space. The slow pointer moves 1 step at a time; the fast pointer moves 2. If they meet, there's a cycle.

Finding cycle start: After meeting, reset one pointer to head. Move both at speed 1 — they meet exactly at the cycle's start node. This works due to the mathematical relationship between the meeting point and cycle entry.
sections.codeBlock:
def has_cycle(head) -> bool:
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow is fast:  return True  # use 'is' not '=='
    return False

def find_cycle_start(head):
    slow = fast = head
    # Phase 1: detect meeting point
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow is fast:
            break
    else:
        return None  # no cycle

    # Phase 2: find cycle start
    # Math: distance from head to cycle_start == distance from meeting point to cycle_start
    slow = head
    while slow is not fast:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next
    return slow  # cycle start node

def cycle_length(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow is fast:
            # Count steps back to meeting point
            length = 1
            fast = fast.next
            while slow is not fast:
                fast = fast.next
                length += 1
            return length
    return 0

sections.title: Slow/Fast Pointer Patterns
sections.description: The slow/fast pointer pattern solves many classic problems elegantly in O(N) / O(1). Key insight: fast moves at 2× — when fast reaches end, slow is at middle.
sections.codeBlock:
# Middle of linked list
def find_middle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    return slow  # slow is at (N//2) position

# Palindrome linked list — O(N) time, O(1) space
def is_palindrome(head):
    # 1. Find middle
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next
    # 2. Reverse second half
    prev, cur = None, slow
    while cur:
        nxt = cur.next; cur.next = prev; prev = cur; cur = nxt
    # 3. Compare first and second halves
    left, right = head, prev
    while right:  # second half is shorter or equal
        if left.val != right.val: return False
        left = left.next; right = right.next
    return True

# Nth node from end (two-pointer gap trick)
def nth_from_end(head, n):
    fast = slow = head
    for _ in range(n): fast = fast.next   # advance fast by N
    while fast:                            # move both until fast hits end
        slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next
    return slow

sections.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists & Merge K Lists
sections.description: Merging is a fundamental building block — used in merge sort, database joins, and external sort algorithms.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• deque — Double-ended queue — Python's deque: O(1) append/pop at both ends, Python's answer to linked list needs
  • Linked Lists in Python — Real Python tutorial with implementation and interview patterns
  • LeetCode — Linked List Problems — Curated linked list interview problems
  • NeetCode (Linked List) — Categorized FAANG linked list problems with video solutions
  • Cracking the Coding Interview (6th ed.) — Chapter 2: linked lists — classic interview patterns

details:
Dummy Head Node: Always use a dummy (sentinel) node when building or modifying a linked list — it eliminates special-case handling for empty lists and head node insertion/deletion. Return dummy.next as the result. This pattern appears in merge, reverse, remove nth node, etc.

In-place vs extra space: Most linked list problems can be solved in O(1) space (no new nodes, just re-linking). If you find yourself creating a new list, ask if re-linking solves it. The interviewer will notice.

XOR Linked List: A memory-efficient doubly linked list stores XOR of prev and next pointers instead of two separate pointers. O(1) space for each direction. Academic curiosity — Python's GC doesn't guarantee pointer stability, so this is impractical in Python but common in C interview questions.
